sentences.text: I-- I was speaking about the selection.
My mother and sister went, first of all to different camp.
The danger was always, are they really going to different camps?
Are they really taking them to work?
Maybe they go straight to the gas chambers, like the transport before us.
But the German changed the policy.
And they needed people.
And therefore, they made the selection.
Before they didn't care and killed everyone.
Now, they went to a near-- near to us, to a so-called Frauen Konzen, FKL, in the Birkenau compound.
And from there, my sister and mother wrote me a letter, which is extraordinary.
But because I knew very well a kapo from there-- I mentioned before, and he smuggled the letter out, and brought it to me, still to the Familienlager, and it was a horrible letter.
It said, impossible conditions, terribly suffering for them.
Because later on, I saw this FKL camp.
It was after the Familien camp.
It was 10 times worse, impossible conditions.
Anyhow, they were sent later on to Germany.
They finished in Stutthof, and the very few years after the war, I knew that they'd perished.
But I met a woman who had also a sister.
And she and her sister and my mother and my sister were on the same block.
And she witnessed how one after another they died.
And she could give me a detailed report about it.
This is Mrs.
Bachner from [INAUDIBLE] Street.
She had a jewelry shop after the war.
Now she's an elderly lady.
Bachner.
So that was my mother and sister.
My father was left.
Now, we were left in the camp, only old people, mothers with little children, and that was in the-- maybe in the middle of-- at the end of June, beginning of July, the selection.
Then at this time, the interment time, was like a dreamlike time.
We had not anymore appel.
Because we were written off.
I mean, until they will be placed in the crematorium, they will put the rest, the old and the children, and mothers with children to the crematorium.
And we knew it.
But on the other hand, there was a freedom.
There was a kind of little [NON-ENGLISH] near the kitchen, a water supply.
Nobody was--
A pool, a kind-- yeah.
Nobody was allowed to go near to it.
At this few-- at maybe 10 days or so, we could even have a bath there.
We were completely safe.
It was summer.
And we could do whatever we wanted.
It was unbelievable.
It wasn't anarchy, but suddenly there was no law.
I told you there was a law as long with being protected at no schools, Theresienstadt, school in underground.
In Auschwitz, like to hell with them.
They can do what they like, if it suits our purpose.
So we had this kind of-- as if school.
Even a tiny little library, officially.
Now this--
Nothing.
No appel.
Sorry?
Later on-- no, no no.
We were close in our compound, in this Czech Familienlager.
Then there were two selections, for which reason we don't know.
We think-- there are many theories.
That maybe this one woman who Mengele or Schwarzhuber was keen of-- asked to save some children.
Some people think they needed some working force of young people or to help.
The other theory, the International Red Cross at that time, the foreign minister in Germany, had some talks with other-- other foreign ministry, maybe to make a separate peace with America.
And they said, well, we can't do it.
The American Jews want some-- [INAUDIBLE] there are still Jews.
Otherwise there will be no kind of talks.
So maybe they wanted to-- to have us for us for this purpose.
Anyhow, there was a selection, only boys between about 12, 13 till 14, 15.
The first selection was done by Hauptsturmfuhrer Schwarzhuber, who was main SS man of Birkenau.
Mengele had a completely different-- he was also Hauptsturmfuhrer.
But he was only medical and selection, things like that.
And Schwarzhuber was the head of Birkenau.
There was also Hirsch and Kramer, but they were--
No, Schwarzhuber wasn't a doctor, SS, a very cruel one.
He had done most of the selections.
Oh, he hardly killed somebody himself.
There was smaller charges who have done it.
And Schwarzhuber made a selection of about 90 children.
And how did it went?
We went-- we had to be naked.
Of course, a number, and again, left and right.
And whoever was there, he was told he can go.
Now, we had a kind of as-if freedom to choose, to choose what?
To go with our parents or whoever was there into the gas chamber, let's say with my father or other people with their parents, or until whatever was left, or to try our luck.
Now, let's say somebody choose to go out the very first time, or opposite-- a few days later, Mengele made another selection.
Maybe Mengele didn't like it, and said, Schwarzhuber took his job.
So he made a second selection, and more or less the same number of boys were selected.
Again, we had as if the choice to go at all to this selection or not.
Some of them changed their attitude.
Some-- the parents say, let's die together.
Yeah, so there were tremendously great dilemmas what to do.
And tragedies, of course.
But we didn't know it.
My father knew exactly what will happen.
Why?
He had one of gold fillings.
And he knew that gold will be taken, and he wanted-- which was loose, and he could take it out.
He said, Yehudah, take it.
Anyhow, someone will take it from you.
You can buy a piece of bread.
Of course, I refused.
But only to say he knew exactly, like all of us.
What's going-- everybody knew exactly the details.
Yeah.
I knew-- like when my mother went.
And so I encouraged her, said you have to be strong.
And I will survive.
And I knew I will not survive.
I was so small and weak physically.
The same was now the second selection.
Both selection I passed.
I went to both selections, and I said, adieu, adieu, to my father.
I wasn't allowed to cry.
I had to be very tough.
I had to tell him, father, we will meet again.
Don't worry.
I am clever.
I am strong.
I will survive.
But I knew what will happen to him.
He knew the chances are very, very, very little.
We will meet again in Israel.
By the way, even before similar things happened, when we were still children in the block with other people, I remember the people who were just dying.
They put on their baton for counting, for many reasons.
First of all, you wanted to have an additional--
Addition, another-- and for me, it was curious to see somebody dying.
I remember there was an old German Jew.
He could speak only in German.
His name was [?
Tauber. ?] And he was just-- you know, in the agony, choking.
And he said, please, please, I'm dying.
I have two sons in a kibbutz in Israel.
Give them regards from my father-- from their father.
And I said, of course I will do it, and give me your name.
And I knew I will not survive it.
But I listened to him.
And somehow I had compassion, not knowingly, some intuitive reflection.
It was interesting for me, old men I've never seen such a thing.
So I knelt next to him.
And I heard him-- what he wanted.
And of course, later he died.
And-- but why I wanted to tell it to, we-- we had still some humanity at that time and feelings.
That was before we-- we were separated from our parents.
Now, the day came.
There was a selection.
They called our number after the selection.
There were some very tragic thing.
Because one man who had to go to work, who was our teacher, the same teacher who taught us about the French Revolution, they miss-- they missed.
Somehow we send the number.
And they couldn't take him.
Because it was a different number.
So he said, oh, I was-- I had always bad luck.
And you see now I'm staying here, meaning I'm going to the gas.
It was terrible for me.
But things like that happened.
We went out of the camp.
We didn't know what it will be.
It was the afternoon.
Some Polish, one or two--
Yeah.
Then they called us.
It was the advance after a day or two.
They called the numbers.
And we went--
No, no, no, no.
The [NON-ENGLISH] and there came a Polish couple.
No.
Only this group of 89--
89 children.
But the rest were left in the camp.
And we went out with the couple, who was quite nice to us.
And we were marching.
And we-- we-- we didn't know where we marching.
The next camp was a C camp, the woman camp.
Then came the main lager.
And we knew, and we went further on.
And we went into the Zigeunerlager.
Gypsy.
And we didn't know the gypsies.
It was the very first time I was there.
We were happy we didn't went in the crematoria straight.
But what was the reason we went to the gypsy camp-- they put us to the very end of the camp.
There was the block of Dr.
Mengele.
And there we were waiting.
I remember we saw some twins.
I remember even the door with 'Dr.
Mengele' written on.
Next to us were some twins.
And we were waiting.
And then we had a shower.
In Auschwitz, always when you went from one camp to another, you went through the sauna, through this kind of ritual eine laus.
Eine laus, how you would call it?
Off the clothes and disinfection.
But everyone was afraid.
Because disinfection in Auschwitz could mean gas.
But us, they could also put in the big disinfection, when-- where we have been when we arrived.
But this was so occupied.
It was for large numbers.
We were only 100, less than 100.
So we-- they took us to that Zigeunerlager, where Mengele had a little disinfection place for his--
For his children, I mean his experimental children.
There we had our sauna.
And I remember as I told you, the gypsies, the children made fun of us.
And said, ah, you see.
The chimney was very near.
You could really see.
That's there you will go.
They will make marmalade or bread from you.
We had more experiences with gypsies.
Anyhow, we--
No, no, no.
The gypsies were living-- they made mostly like if they were twins, Mengele took twins from all over, and all ages and midgets.
From this sauna, where we had this little sauna.
That was a reason why we went to the Zigeunerlager.
We came to main lager.
The main lager was the main camp, where all the main centers of jobs and so on were in the camps.
Kanada-- Kanada was these people who worked with the--
In the platforms, and they had much better food.
They were all the great kapos.
People went out of Auschwitz for work.
And they were in much better position.
Yes, in Zigeunerlager we were only for, let's say, a couple of hours.
And then we came to the main--
But in the main lager, there were, at my time three blocks which were isolated.
It was a normal lager.
But three blocks were isolated, had a wall with bricks.
And that was number 9, 11, and 13.
And number 9 and 11 were the sonderkommando.
And they had special washrooms.
They had even a little ambulance if somebody was sick a little.
So they were living in the beginning in the blocks, in number 9 and 11.
In the highest of the Hungarian transports were about 1,200 workers for the crematorium.
And in 13, where we have been, was also a closed block.
We had a special lavatory, a much better one, a luxury one for kapos.
A lavatory, yeah.
Really, and a private, it's private.
I mean there was a real you douche, a real toilet, pissoir.
But only the kapo and sometimes even the lager kapo, Danish, came and made his things there.
And we children, we came there.
We also were washed there, another shower.
And then we came to block number 13.
The block was so-called SK.
So now there are SK, SK, sonderkommando.
And SK 13 meant strafkommando, the special-- Punishment.
--punishment called.
But there people who has done something very wrong in Auschwitz-Birkenau were put there, one of the worst places, that had terribly hard labor, and from the early morning until the late afternoon.
We were in the same block.
But in our time, meaning July '44, conditions were much better in all Auschwitz.
In-- in '40-- sorry-- '40-- yes, I said '44.
They told me in '42 and '43 it was unbearable to live.
I mean they killed everyone.
It was horrible.
In '44 at least killing just everyone or this kind of terror didn't exist anymore, for many reasons.
I can go in details if you like.
But now we came to this strafkommando, but not as punishment, but for isolation.
And why, it was unknown.
Anyhow we were received.
And it was like a dream.
These 89--
89 boys.
Everything was terribly clean, orderly.
Food was on time.
The blockalteste, Bednarek was his name, his number was 1,325, or something like this.
He was Polish-- a German, how do they call it?
Volksdeutsche, a German from Poland, who was born in Poland and so on.
But all these Volksdeutsche who behaved a little for the Nazi, I mean a better way.
They could even had their own hair, this special kind of treatment for the better.
I heard that Bednarek was a quite murderer.
And when this transport, our Czech transport went to the gas, on the 7th of March, he was one of them who had to beat them, and put them on this--
Push them on the-- and there were terrible scenes, which we knew later about.
Anyhow, to us children, he was very correct.
He even somehow was boosting with us.
We had to march and sing Czech songs.
And he went with us to the kleiderkammer.
Kleiderkammer was a special block only with cloth.
And the very first time in Auschwitz, we got shoes according to your--
To your number, and normal shoes which was unbelievable and unheard.
So our treatment was-- we didn't know what will happen.
Will they take us to Germany, or send us, or what will happen we don't know.
This lageralteste, Danish, he was a very great anti-Semite.
And he came often to our block, and he with hate said, you children, soon we will take off your hair.
And you will work like everyone else.
I mean he waited--
At that time, yes.
Later on, he succeeded, let's say, took off our hair.
He-- we went to a job.
Our job was-- instead of horses there was a [NON-ENGLISH], a kind of--
Of a-- yeah.
And we were pushing it.
There were two or three kinds of, how you call it?
A-- a wagon, sort of, you know, instead of horses--
We pulled it, yeah.
Well, it was all kinds of things.
We called it roll wagon, a-- a wagon which rolled.
And we were going with it.
We had a little kapo with us, or a blockalteste, who took us from one camp to another, and all kinds of odd jobs.
We went to the train.
Sometimes we took the food from the valises which were left.
Sometimes we took the blankets.
And sometimes we took a bicycle which went out of order from the German or a motorbike.
Like, you know they carry for--
Just like you see in a village even today in Poland, and so on.
About 20.
And there were two or three roll wagons.
Even the musicians-- well, I will speak later, but the orchestra in Auschwitz that also later on, they had such a kind of roll wagon.
So that was our job.
Sometimes we went with a motorbike of SS man to Auschwitz [INAUDIBLE] to repair.
So we went there, and we could see everything.
All over.
There was not one camp I haven't seen.
And many things happened.
Of course, sometimes the kapo or blockalteste used us only for some rendezvous of a nice Hungarian kapo in the camp of the woman.
And he said, come with us.
And now do this.
And he meanwhile went to-- to this nice, pretty girl.
And after a half our or so, he said, OK.
Now we go back.
So we were also a cover for many things for these people who went with us.
But we have done all kinds of little jobs.
And I remember once we went to a-- to the Hungarian compound where the Hungarian people had lived.
And all these Hungarian women came to us and said, you're a kitchie, kitchie.
My kitchie-- it's a little, little baby.
You are my son.
And it was heartbreaking.
Maybe you can look for our son.
And we tried to help.
But we asked.
We were very clever how old was-- were-- was your son.
If he was under 14 or so, there was no hope that he is alive.
But we never told it to them, to these women, what was there.
Even to me it happened that a cousin, a woman about at that time, about 40-ish or so, 30-ish, 30-plus, and had two young daughters arrived in this Zigeunerlager.
And somehow it came out that she is my relative.
She was living in Berlin.
Then she came via--
But she was not in Zigeunerlager.
No, no, no.
She-- No.
Zigeunerlager was E lager.
I speak now about the C compound, where there were Hungarian woman.
Yes.
And someone from your family arrived.
Yeah.
I didn't know her from before.
But it came out that we are cousins.
And what could I do for her?
There were two young girls.
And we had at that time-- you know, we were going around with our wagon to all of the camps.
All over.
And how old were the girls?
The girls must have been 13 or 15, something like that, two daughters.
Both of them are alive.
Even their mother survived, but she later died.
And as we went to this huge camp of Kanada, where there were enormous huge places with a lot of clothes, from all kinds of-- they were sorting it and so on.
So I could get hold of the best, the nicest pieces, and for them I smuggled in a brassiere and lipstick.
Because that saved them.
I told them you must go as quick as out of Auschwitz.
You must look young.
This little rouge and the brassiere helps them.
I brought them some sandwiches.
Because at that time, the Hungarian Jews brought a lot of food.
And through the sonderkommando we got some additional food.
And that helped me in a way to supply them.
What it meant at that time, a sandwich, you can't imagine.
Anyhow, that was these people.
So that was one of our jobs.
Once, I remember we went in the same C compound with Hungarian Jews.
And there was a very famous, very bad SS woman.
They had most of the SS women.
And she saw me.
I was maybe a pretty child.
Maybe I reminded her--
At that-- at that time I was about 14, yeah, exactly 14.
And she said, come here.
And she gave me a pot with noodles.
I mean a huge-- and said, eat it.
And I was completely--
Well, I was a little boy.
But we were in much, comparatively much better conditions than others.
And you see, the German-- that was always crazy.
Yes, she could before kill a few women.
But suddenly she saw me, and she said, come in.
And she liked you.
So she gave you--
And she-- it was a one time.
Of course, I mean hungry we were, but not as hungry as the other one.
That was one of these--
No.
Normal, you couldn't be.
But I could keep a more or less human frame.
Yes.
And being more or less in good shape makes a tremendous difference.
I wasn't a Muselmann, let's say.
So things like that happened.
I remember another case-- so now we were in this strafkommando, but not as prisoners--
Not punished people, but as isolated.
Because they thought it's the best way to keep us separated.
Some of our boys were sent to be people for the SS, call boys for the SS in that Zigeunerlager, which later saved my life.
Two of them are still alive or three of them.
Some were sent to different places.
Some were [NON-ENGLISH], which was a very high kind of functioner.
Running around, but this [NON-ENGLISH], it's unbelievable if I say it today, they had power over life and death about all the prisoners.
If he was a bad [NON-ENGLISH], for example--
Yeah.
He could take the whole ration of the whole day of the coffee and spill it.
And the people would be without food.
Or vice versa, I remember we tried to help and we took bread and we-- later on Czech people came, and we distributed it.
Or when children from Lodz came--
Well, a little less.
Because one boy found his father, Kulka, who is today a professor at the university.
And so he was taken to his father who had a kind of workshop.
And he was staying with him.
And as I told you, others were [NON-ENGLISH] and so on.
